completeness.rationale: Captured 6 of 7 expected items: task count (~5,000), title-fast/description-slow distinction, v2.3 regression trigger, ~2 weeks ago onset, 10-15 second duration, and long descriptions. The one missing item is CPU spike behavior during searches, which was never asked about.

accuracy.rationale: The triage accurately identified both acceptable diagnostic paths simultaneously: the v2.3 upgrade as the regression trigger AND the title-vs-description performance split pointing to an indexing issue. The hypothesis of 'dropping or failing to use an index on description content, switching to a non-indexed scan' maps directly to the actual cause (FTS5 → LIKE query regression). No inaccuracies present.

thoroughness.rationale: Three well-structured rounds of questioning covered the key diagnostic space. The agent missed one notable follow-up: asking about CPU or resource usage during searches, which would have surfaced the single-core 100% spike and strengthened the full-table-scan hypothesis. Otherwise resolved with a solid basis.

economy.rationale: All three question turns were productive and non-redundant. Turn 1 established baseline and timeline, turn 2 elicited reproduction steps and discovered the title/description split, turn 3 gathered environment and scale. No wasted turns. Could have combined OS/environment questions with an earlier turn but the sequencing was logical.

question_quality.rationale: The turn 2 question — asking for a concrete walkthrough of reproduction steps — was particularly effective. It prompted the reporter to surface the title-vs-description distinction unprompted ('actually, now that I think about it'), which is exactly the kind of diagnostic gold that reporters don't volunteer without structured prompting. Questions were specific and well-framed throughout.

actionability.rationale: The summary is exceptionally actionable: exact reproduction steps with task count and description length requirements, a pinpointed hypothesis (index regression on description path in v2.3), a recommended investigation path (compare query plans, check what changed in v2.3), a proposed test case with acceptance criteria, and identified information gaps. A developer could start coding or investigating immediately.

notable_strengths: Turn 2 structuring of reproduction steps successfully elicited the title-vs-description performance split, which the reporter wouldn't have volunteered otherwise — this was the key diagnostic insight
Root cause hypothesis accurately converged on both acceptable diagnostic paths (version regression + indexing issue) without being given either clue directly
Triage summary is unusually complete: includes reproduction steps, environment, hypothesis, recommended fix, proposed test case, and explicit information gaps
notable_weaknesses: Never asked about CPU or system resource behavior during slow searches — the 100% single-core spike would have strongly confirmed a full-table-scan hypothesis
Did not ask the reporter to check app logs or console output, which might have surfaced query timing or warning messages
most_insightful_question: Could you walk me through the steps to reproduce the slow search? [...] What are you typing into the search box [...] Does it happen with every search query, or only certain ones?
missed_opportunities: Ask whether CPU or memory usage spikes visibly during slow searches (would surface the single-core 100% spike)
Ask whether the desktop app exposes any logs or a debug console that might show query timing or errors
Ask whether performance degrades linearly with query length or is uniformly slow regardless of search term length
